questions.text: I find that my tendency to approach suffering analytically has allowed me to be helpful in situations where others felt paralyzed.
I find that the thought of keeping something for myself while others lack is deeply uncomfortable.
I do not pay much attention to shifts in someone's posture or body positioning.
I find that I do not naturally read subtext in emails or text messages.
I feel that the moral pull of another person's pain creates an imperative in me that no amount of reasoning can fully override or dismiss.
I think of authority as something to be shared, not concentrated.
I hold the view that self-investment is not selfish—it's essential.
I pick up on when a thank-you carries genuine gratitude versus social routine.
I notice that my body stays relaxed in situations that make other people tense.
I think that the most productive conversations about human tragedy are those conducted with analytical precision rather than emotional intensity.
I prefer to live forward without spending much time documenting or reviewing the past.
I would rather be a key early contributor than the founder.
I feel that my emotional intensity is not something I can simply decide to turn off.
I hold the view that my low reactivity is not a deficit — it's a different way of being in the world.
I maintain organized systems for managing my files, finances, and personal records.
I feel that my assertiveness is one of my greatest assets.
I find that too many procedures slow things down and prevent creative problem-solving.
I hold the view that taking the best opportunity for myself is simply good judgment.
